video_title: The Ethereum Foundation Is Splitting Up - The Chopping Block
gist: The Chopping Block panel (Dragonfly's Steve/Haseeb, Gauntlet's Tarun, DeFi's Tom, plus journalist Laura Shin) argues Strategy's STRC 'Stretch' preferred has de-pegged from $100 to ~$87 on a confidence (not solvency) shock, debates how close it is to a Luna-style spiral, dissects the Ethereum Foundation spinning out ETH Labs amid 20% layoffs, frames CME's lawsuit against the CFTC over perps as protectionism doomed by crypto's whack-a-mole structuring, and reads Meta's 'Arena' prediction-market test as an eyeballs/ad-data play rather than a Polymarket killer.

insights.claim: Strategy's STRC 'Stretch' preferred shares, designed to trade near a $100 price target with an original ~10.5% dividend, have de-pegged to roughly $85-87, implying the market now demands closer to a 14% yield to hold them.
insights.speaker: Steve (Haseeb Qureshi, Dragonfly)
insights.so_what: A preferred trading well below its $100 target is a real-time market gauge of perceived blow-up risk in the largest Bitcoin treasury vehicle; watch STRC price as a sentiment proxy for the whole MSTR stack.
insights.quote: Went all the way down to 82 cents. Now, it seems to have stabilized around 86 85.

insights.claim: Strategy holds about $1.4 billion in cash as of June 22nd against roughly $1.5 billion of annual STRC dividends owed, giving it only about one year of dividend coverage from cash if it issues no more Stretch.
insights.speaker: Steve (Haseeb Qureshi, Dragonfly)
insights.so_what: Thin cash-to-dividend coverage means Strategy must keep tapping common-equity issuance or Bitcoin sales to service the preferred, each of which pressures another part of the cap stack.
insights.quote: They do have, I believe, 1.4 billion in cash as of June 22nd... every year they have something like a billion5 um roughly of dividends that they're owed on the outstanding stretch.

insights.claim: The STRC de-peg is driven by a collapse in confidence in Strategy's financial judgment rather than literal insolvency, after management paid down 2029 debt early and sold 32 Bitcoin for only ~$2.5 million.
insights.speaker: Laura Shin (host/journalist)
insights.so_what: If the de-peg is a confidence/judgment story, small nominal management missteps can move the price far more than the cash position warrants, making headline risk the key thing to monitor.
insights.quote: They sold the 32 bitcoin for for what reason? It was like again it just shook the market's confidence. You know it's like such a nominal amount of money two and a half million like it doesn't move the needle on anything.

insights.claim: Tarun frames Strategy as the potential 'Luna for suits,' arguing the mechanism of continuously diluting common shareholders to defend a pegged target mirrors Luna's adaptive inflation, just executed discretely via convert events rather than continuously on-chain.
insights.speaker: Tarun (Gauntlet)
insights.so_what: Reframing MSTR's converts/preferreds as a securitized-Bitcoin reflexivity machine gives investors a mental model for how confidence loss could feed dilution and price pressure.
insights.quote: I have Luna inflation. The only difference is like I'm doing it continuously on chain versus like I'm doing it discreetly via these like convert events.

insights.claim: Haseeb rejects the Luna comparison as existential, arguing Strategy holds ~$55 billion of Bitcoin and Saylor can simply defer STRC dividends indefinitely, so there are no forced margin calls or mandatory death-spiral dynamics.
insights.speaker: Steve (Haseeb Qureshi, Dragonfly)
insights.so_what: If dividend deferral is a real escape valve, a STRC blow-up is not automatically a forced-seller event for Bitcoin, capping the contagion thesis many traders fear.
insights.quote: He defers the dividends indefinitely. And he can do that. He has the right to defer the the dividends until infinity. That means he never gets to raise stretch again.

insights.claim: The panel argues a STRC failure would be a one-time hit to Bitcoin (removing a marginal buyer) rather than a recurring spiral, because Strategy has no obligation to sell Bitcoin and can abandon Stretch like 'burning the boat.'
insights.speaker: Steve (Haseeb Qureshi, Dragonfly)
insights.so_what: Distinguishing a one-time marginal-buyer removal from a reflexive death spiral helps size the downside to Bitcoin from a Strategy preferred failure.
insights.quote: That is a one-time event. He doesn't have to sell any more Bitcoin... There's no death spiral. There's no margin calls. There's no nothing. That's why it's not like Luna.

insights.claim: The closest real-world analogy to STRC's situation is an Argentina-style sovereign default rather than a Luna collapse, a manageable distressed-debt event with workout options rather than an inevitable wipeout.
insights.speaker: Tom (DeFi)
insights.so_what: Pricing STRC like distressed sovereign debt (recovery value, deferral risk) rather than a stablecoin peg gives a cleaner valuation lens for the preferred.
insights.quote: It's more like a Argentina default versus a Luna moment.

insights.claim: Strategy reportedly called up random crypto funds and money managers last week trying to sell Stretch, behavior the panel read as a Luna-style distress signal even while deeming the underlying problem manageable.
insights.speaker: guest, panelist (Dragonfly)
insights.so_what: Issuers cold-calling non-natural buyers for a preferred is a classic late-stage liquidity tell worth tracking as a leading indicator of stress.
insights.quote: last week they were calling up random different you know funds and people in crypto that manage money trying to sell them stretch people who you probably would not normally call

insights.claim: Raising the preferred dividend to defend STRC indirectly dilutes Strategy's common shareholders, so every move management makes harms some stakeholder in the cap stack, leaving them boxed into a corner.
insights.speaker: Haseeb / Tarun (Dragonfly / Gauntlet)
insights.so_what: Because defending the preferred dilutes the common and selling Bitcoin pressures the asset, MSTR common and preferred holders face opposed interests an investor must pick a side on.
insights.quote: no matter what they do they're going to hurt one part of their little empire.

themes.theme: Ethereum Foundation Spins Out ETH Labs

insights.claim: ETH Labs launched as a nonprofit spin-out of seven senior Ethereum Foundation members (including Barnaby, Josh Stark, Ansgar) one day before the EF announced layoffs of ~20% of headcount (50+ people), signaling a deliberate decentralization of power away from the EF.
insights.speaker: Drew (host, Dragonfly)
insights.so_what: A structural reorganization of Ethereum's core stewardship toward adoption/markets could shift how protocol and tokenomics decisions get made, with downstream relevance for ETH.
insights.quote: the EF announced layoffs where it was laying off roughly 20% of the headcount of the Ethereum Foundation which is about 50 plus people.

insights.claim: ETH Labs is being funded by Ethereum DATs (digital asset treasury companies) including Bitmine, Sharplink and Joe Lubin personally, plus SNZ, tying the new org to commercial ETH-treasury backers rather than the EF's original ICO endowment.
insights.speaker: Drew (host, Dragonfly)
insights.so_what: Commercial DAT funding of Ethereum's adoption arm aligns ETH price/treasury interests with protocol development, a potential catalyst for ETH-focused capital allocation.
insights.quote: Ethlabs is also being funded by the DATs to bring it back to that conversation. I believe uh Bitmine as well as um Sharlink and Joe Luben personally are among the biggest backers of this group as well as SNZ.

insights.claim: Laura Shin argues the EF reorganization was forced by runway pressure: with ETH's price down, the foundation lacked money to sustain headcount, making layoffs a financial necessity rather than purely a governance signal.
insights.speaker: Laura Shin (host/journalist)
insights.so_what: If EF cuts are runway-driven, ETH price weakness directly constrains Ethereum's core funding, linking token performance to ecosystem development capacity.
insights.quote: I'm talking about their runway like I think they did this because they didn't have enough money.

insights.claim: Laura Shin contends the EF historically neglected ETH tokenomics and that Vitalik's blog post calling ETH the highest-value product of the chain was insincere lip service to community demands rather than genuine conviction.
insights.speaker: Laura Shin (host/journalist)
insights.so_what: A founder team that deprioritizes token economics is a structural bear factor for ETH relative to chains that actively manage value accrual.
insights.quote: even though Vitalik made that blog post saying like ETH was the most high value product of the Ethereum blockchain, like I sort of felt like he was paying lip service.

insights.claim: The panel frames the EF/ETH Labs split as the EF retreating to a 'keep the religion alive' mandate (privacy, security, open source, core values) while ETH Labs takes on adoption, scaling, the EVM and protocol economics, with the EF still funding ETH Labs.
insights.speaker: Drew / Steve (Dragonfly)
insights.so_what: A two-headed Ethereum governance structure introduces negotiation friction over a fixed resource pool, a coordination risk for the pace of upgrades and adoption.
insights.quote: the EF is basically saying we don't want to do that we are only going to keep the religion alive we're going to protect the core values.

insights.claim: Tarun criticizes the ETH Labs launch as an 'announcement of an announcement' lacking concrete 90-day deliverables, arguing the absence of accountable, time-bound goals is exactly the leadership Ethereum has been missing.
insights.speaker: Tarun (Gauntlet)
insights.so_what: Lack of a concrete roadmap signals execution risk; watch whether ETH Labs publishes accountable sprints before crediting it with revitalizing Ethereum.
insights.quote: it just felt a little announcement of an announcement.

insights.claim: The panel expects ongoing conflict between the EF and ETH Labs because 'go fast' adoption (shipping upgrades, higher performance) trades off against decentralization and core values, with the EF retaining leverage through funding of client teams.
insights.speaker: Steve (Haseeb Qureshi, Dragonfly)
insights.so_what: EF control over client-team funding means it can still veto ETH Labs' direction, so adoption-driven catalysts for ETH may be slower than the spin-out narrative implies.
insights.quote: the EF has enormous amount of influence over, you know, the the client teams and the impleers. I mean, a lot of them are getting funded by the EF.

themes.theme: CME Sues CFTC Over Crypto Perps

insights.claim: CME, which controls roughly 93% of US exchange-traded derivatives, sued its own regulator the CFTC to block approval of perpetual futures from Kalshi and Coinbase, arguing perps are legally swaps (not futures) and thus improperly approved.
insights.speaker: Drew (host, Dragonfly)
insights.so_what: If reclassified as swaps, US-listed crypto perps would face a different regulatory regime and lose their futures licenses, reshaping the domestic perp market.
insights.quote: they own something like 93% of derivatives traded in the US.

insights.claim: The CFTC publicly called CME's lawsuit frivolous and characterized it as an incumbent using 'lawfare' to protect a near-monopoly, unusually aggressive language toward the largest US derivatives venue.
insights.speaker: Drew (host, Dragonfly)
insights.so_what: An openly hostile regulator stance toward CME raises the odds the perp approvals survive, supporting Coinbase/Kalshi's product roadmaps.
insights.quote: the CFTC has fought back saying that they believe the lawsuit is frivolous and that this is basically an incumbent using lawfare.

insights.claim: Tarun predicts the case will hinge on the technical detail of perps' periodic funding payments, which give them a continuously-repriced market price unlike a fixed-date swap, making perps look more like futures than swaps.
insights.speaker: Tarun (Gauntlet)
insights.so_what: The funding-mechanism argument is the likely legal fulcrum; its resolution determines whether perps stay in the lighter-touch futures regime.
insights.quote: computing the funding all the time doing it periodically does that give it sort of like a a real price that like a swap might not have.

insights.claim: The panel argues CME's suit is structurally futile because even if perps are deemed swaps, crypto firms will route around it by restructuring (e.g., Coinbase already offers ~5-year-rollover futures that mimic perps), creating more derivatives, not fewer.
insights.speaker: Steve / Tarun (Dragonfly / Gauntlet)
insights.so_what: A 'whack-a-mole' structuring response means CME likely cannot durably block perps, so the litigation is unlikely to be a lasting moat for its derivatives franchise.
insights.quote: I think the CME going after this is ironically going to create many more. It's like a whack-a-ole like they by trying to kill one they created 10 more.

insights.claim: The real driver of CME's lawsuit is that its stock price falls as Hyperliquid volume rises, with Hyperliquid coming up repeatedly on CME's earnings calls and dominating media coverage despite being a tiny fraction of CME's actual volume.
insights.speaker: Steve (Haseeb Qureshi, Dragonfly)
insights.so_what: Hyperliquid's outsized narrative impact on CME shareholder perception is a tradable signal of where on-chain perp share is migrating, even before it shows up in volumes.
insights.quote: their stock price just keeps going down when hyperlid volume goes up... hyperlquid comes up like a bunch of times. CEOs getting very annoyed about having to answer all those questions.

insights.claim: Steve frames the CME suit as the inverse of Coinbase's SEC suit: Coinbase sued for the right to compete, while CME is suing the CFTC for the right not to compete.
insights.speaker: Steve (Haseeb Qureshi, Dragonfly)
insights.so_what: Framing the suit as anti-competitive lawfare clarifies the regulatory-narrative risk for incumbents and the tailwind for crypto-native venues.
insights.quote: it's like the inverse of the Coinbase lawsuit where Coinbase sued the SEC for the right to compete and the CME is suing the CFTC for the right to not compete.

insights.claim: A CFTC commissioner publicly signaled limits on perpetual futures, saying physically-delivered commodities like agricultural products should not be perpetual, indicating perp approvals won't be unlimited across all commodities.
insights.speaker: Tarun (Gauntlet)
insights.so_what: Expect carve-outs (especially ag products under lobby pressure), so the perp-expansion thesis applies mainly to financial/crypto underliers, not all commodities.
insights.quote: well not all commodities should be perpetual futures like things that have physical delivery.

insights.claim: Tom notes US/Kalshi perps have been unexpectedly unpopular, with Kalshi perps showing wash-trading at 100x-200x open interest and Coinbase products capped at ~3x-6x leverage, making non-US decentralized perps superior for many users.
insights.speaker: Tom (DeFi)
insights.so_what: Low leverage and weak adoption on US perps suggest offshore/decentralized venues retain the active-trader base regardless of the lawsuit's outcome.
insights.quote: the couchy pers right they're just like obviously wash traded you know they're trading at like 100x 200x open interest.

insights.claim: Tom highlights that futures enjoy better tax treatment than swaps, so a swap reclassification of perps would worsen tax outcomes for traders independent of the regulatory regime change.
insights.speaker: Tom (DeFi)
insights.so_what: Tax differential is an under-discussed reason a swap classification would dampen US perp demand even if products technically survive.
insights.quote: swaps are high you know worse tax treatment than futures.

insights.claim: Meta is reportedly testing a prediction market called Arena across Facebook, WhatsApp and Instagram, launching with play-money (not real money) but open to real money later, reportedly on Zuck's personal directive in response to Polymarket and Kalshi.
insights.speaker: Drew (host, Dragonfly)
insights.so_what: A Meta-distributed prediction product could massively expand prediction-market awareness, a potential tailwind or threat to Polymarket/Kalshi depending on monetization intent.
insights.quote: Meta it was just leaked is apparently considering launching its own prediction market in response to Colian poly market called arena.

insights.claim: Tom, a former Meta/Instagram employee, notes Meta has tried prediction markets at least three or four times before, including an internal Manifold-style tool and 2020's 'Forecast' app, which was wound down for weak adoption.
insights.speaker: Tom (DeFi, ex-Meta)
insights.so_what: Meta's repeated failed attempts temper the threat narrative, but consumer products 'don't work until they work,' so prior failure isn't proof this attempt fails.
insights.quote: this is like the third or fourth time that they

insights.claim: Drew argues prediction markets are effectively media companies disguised as trading platforms, with far more users reading the odds for information than trading, making them an information social network rather than a pure exchange.
insights.speaker: Drew (host, Dragonfly)
insights.so_what: If prediction markets are primarily media/information products, their strategic value (and Meta's interest) lies in eyeballs and data, not trading volume, reframing valuation.
insights.quote: I view prediction markets as like media companies that are dressed in the sheep's clothing of like a trading platform because realistically they just have so many more users who are reading it to get information versus people who are trading.

insights.claim: The panel's most plausible read is that Meta wants prediction-market eyeballs and ad-targeting data, not trading revenue, since winning the category is immaterial to Meta's value but owning where information-seeking attention flows fits its core ad business.
insights.speaker: Steve (Haseeb Qureshi, Dragonfly)
insights.so_what: An eyeballs/ad-data motive means Meta doesn't need trading volume to win, and might rationally just be a front-end aggregator rather than building an exchange.
insights.quote: Poly market makes money on the people betting it doesn't make money on the people who are looking is kind of Meta's whole business model.

insights.claim: The panel suggests Meta's play-money prediction product could act as an accelerant for Polymarket, Kalshi and other venues by globally educating users about prediction markets, since odds data is not banned even where real-money betting is.
insights.speaker: Steve (Haseeb Qureshi, Dragonfly)
insights.so_what: Rather than a competitive threat, Meta's distribution could expand the total addressable market for real-money prediction venues operating outside the US.
insights.quote: I can imagine actually that that is an accelerant for poly market call and all the other players around the world.

insights.claim: Meta's history of 'superficial victory' products (Facebook Dating, Marketplace, Workplace) that scale large but lack a natural surface or are wound down makes the panel skeptical Arena clears the stickiness bar despite Meta's near-global platform reach.
insights.speaker: Steve / Tom (Dragonfly / ex-Meta)
insights.so_what: Execution and surface-fit risk, not distribution, is the binding constraint on Meta winning prediction markets, lowering the displacement threat to incumbents.
insights.quote: and I think meta products often have this thing where they have this superficial victory like like Facebook dating is actually gigantic, but I don't think any of us know anyone who uses Facebook dating.
